2.2.a.info:
A trunk port is a switch port configured to carry traffic for multiple VLANs simultaneously over a single physical link. Instead of being dedicated to one VLAN the way an access port is, a trunk multiplexes many VLANs onto the same wire and uses 802.1Q tags to mark each frame with its VLAN ID. This lets frames from VLAN 10, VLAN 20, and VLAN 99 ride the same cable between two switches without ever getting mixed up on the other side.

Where trunks appear in a real network: switch-to-switch uplinks (the most common case), switch-to-router links doing Router on a Stick (ROAS), switch-to-WLC links carrying both management and client-side VLANs, and switch-to-hypervisor links where a single NIC carries many VM VLANs. Wherever a link needs to carry more than one broadcast domain, it is a trunk.

How a switch treats a trunk versus an access port: On an access port, incoming frames are assumed to belong to the configured access VLAN and outgoing frames are always sent untagged. On a trunk port, incoming tagged frames are placed in the VLAN indicated by the tag, and outgoing frames for a given VLAN are tagged with that VLAN ID before being transmitted. The one exception is the native VLAN — covered in 2.2.c — whose frames are sent untagged even on a trunk.

Configuration minimum: interface Gi0/1 → switchport mode trunk → switchport nonegotiate → switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30. This combination forces trunking, disables DTP (security), and prunes the trunk to only the VLANs you actually need.

Verification: show interfaces trunk is the single most important command — it lists every trunk port, its mode, encapsulation, native VLAN, and which VLANs are allowed/active/in STP forwarding. show vlan brief does NOT show trunk ports — this is a classic exam trap.

hack.memory: Trunk = multi-lane highway with VLAN lane markers (802.1Q tags). Access = single-lane residential road (one VLAN, no tags). Every tagged frame on a trunk says 'I belong to VLAN X.' The receiving switch respects the tag, strips it if forwarding out an access port, keeps it if forwarding out another trunk. Rule: if the link needs more than one VLAN, it must be a trunk.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: connect two 2960 switches with a crossover. On each side: 'interface Gi0/1', 'switchport mode trunk', 'switchport nonegotiate', 'switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20'. Create VLANs 10 and 20 on both, put a PC in each VLAN on each switch. Ping within VLAN 10 across the trunk — works. Ping from VLAN 10 to VLAN 20 — fails (no L3 yet). Run 'show interfaces trunk' and read every column. Then run 'show vlan brief' and confirm the trunk port does NOT appear — this is the trap.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17 is the canonical trunk video. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 8-9. The exam absolutely tests trunk config and will show 'show interfaces trunk' output — practice parsing the Mode, Encapsulation, Native VLAN, and 'VLANs allowed/active' columns until they are second nature.

2.2.a.info:
switchport mode trunk is the interface-level command that forces a port into trunk mode regardless of what the neighbor wants. Once issued, the port immediately begins tagging frames with 802.1Q on egress and expecting tagged frames on ingress (with the native VLAN as the sole untagged exception). It is the recommended, deterministic way to configure trunks in production networks.

The three possible switchport modes: access (single VLAN, no tagging), trunk (multiple VLANs, tagged), and dynamic (let DTP decide — further split into 'dynamic desirable' and 'dynamic auto'). Forced trunk (mode trunk) is the operational-best-practice choice because DTP introduces security risks and unpredictable behavior.

Critical pairing — switchport nonegotiate: Even when you force mode trunk, the port by default still sends DTP frames every 30 seconds. An attacker with a rogue switch can receive those DTP frames and negotiate a trunk back to your switch, gaining access to every VLAN. Always follow mode trunk with switchport nonegotiate to silence DTP and make the trunk static.

Encapsulation caveat: On older Cisco switches (3560, 3750) that supported both ISL and 802.1Q, you had to first set switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q before switchport mode trunk would work. Modern switches (2960, 9200, 9300) only support 802.1Q, so the encapsulation command is unneeded and often unrecognized. Know it exists — the exam may show it in config.

Order of operations on a 3560-style switch: (1) switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q, (2) switchport mode trunk, (3) switchport nonegotiate, (4) switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20, (5) optional switchport trunk native vlan 999. On a 2960, skip step 1.

hack.memory: The MNA recipe = Mode trunk, Nonegotiate, Allowed vlan list. 'Mode trunk' alone is NOT secure — DTP still whispers out the port. Only switchport nonegotiate makes the trunk silent and static. Think of 'mode trunk' as lifting the gate and 'nonegotiate' as welding it open so no one can negotiate it closed.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: on SwA Gi0/1, configure 'switchport mode trunk' ONLY. Connect SwB Gi0/1 set to 'switchport mode access'. Run 'show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport' on SwA — Administrative Mode = trunk, Operational Mode = trunk (forced). Now add 'switchport nonegotiate' and run 'show dtp interface Gi0/1' on both sides — DTP packets drop to 0. This proves nonegotiate is the real security control.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. The exam will ask 'how do you manually configure a trunk and prevent DTP negotiation?' and the expected answer is 'switchport mode trunk' followed by 'switchport nonegotiate' — both commands, not just one.

2.2.a.info:
switchport trunk allowed vlan controls exactly which VLANs are carried across a trunk link. By default, a trunk allows all VLANs 1-4094 — which is both wasteful (the switch floods unknown unicast and broadcasts for every VLAN over every trunk) and a security risk (attackers could inject traffic into any VLAN that exists on the network). Restricting the allowed list to only the VLANs that need to traverse the link is a fundamental best practice.

Syntax variants — these trip up exam candidates:

  • switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30 — replaces the entire list. This wipes out any previously allowed VLANs and sets the list to exactly 10, 20, and 30. Very easy to break a production trunk by forgetting 'add'.
  • switchport trunk allowed vlan add 40 — appends 40 to the existing list without disturbing the current VLANs.
  • switchport trunk allowed vlan remove 20 — removes 20 from the list.
  • switchport trunk allowed vlan all — resets to the default (all 4094 VLANs allowed).
  • switchport trunk allowed vlan except 30 — allows all VLANs except 30.
  • switchport trunk allowed vlan none — allows zero VLANs (effectively blackhole).

VLANs must exist on both ends: The allowed list is not sufficient on its own. Each VLAN being trunked must also be created in the VLAN database on both switches (vlan 10 → name SALES). If VLAN 20 is allowed on the trunk but does not exist on SwB, traffic tagged with 20 is dropped by SwB — a common troubleshooting scenario.

STP and allowed VLANs: Reducing the allowed list also reduces the scope of the per-VLAN Spanning Tree instances running on that trunk. Pruning unused VLANs off trunks saves CPU in networks with hundreds of VLANs — each blocked/pruned VLAN is one less STP instance to maintain.

Verification: show interfaces trunk displays three columns of interest — 'Vlans allowed on trunk' (config), 'Vlans allowed and active in management domain' (allowed AND exist in VLAN DB), and 'Vlans in spanning tree forwarding state and not pruned' (actually passing traffic right now).

hack.memory: Golden rule: the bare command allowed vlan 10 REPLACES — it does not add. Always use the add keyword when modifying a live trunk in production, or you will wipe the entire list and cause an outage. Memory aid: 'comma-list = clobber, add keyword = append.'
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: on a working trunk carrying VLANs 10 and 20, type 'switchport trunk allowed vlan 30' (no add keyword). Run 'show interfaces trunk' — only 30 is allowed now, and VLANs 10 and 20 traffic is blackholed. This is the accidental outage. Fix with 'switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30' or 'switchport trunk allowed vlan add 10' then 'add 20'. Internalize the difference.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam will show a command and ask what the resulting allowed list is — you must know that the bare syntax replaces and the add/remove/except/all/none keywords each behave differently. This is a favorite exam gotcha.

2.2.a.info:
DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol) is a Cisco-proprietary Layer 2 protocol that two Cisco switches use to automatically negotiate whether a link should become a trunk. DTP runs by default on almost every Cisco switch port — you rarely have to enable it, and you almost always have to explicitly disable it.

How DTP works: A DTP-enabled port sends a DTP frame every 30 seconds announcing its intent: 'I am desirable, I am auto, or I am in trunk mode.' When both sides have compatible intents, the link automatically transitions to trunk without any manual configuration. If the intents are incompatible, the link stays in access mode.

Why DTP exists: In the 1990s and early 2000s, manually configuring every trunk was tedious. DTP let admins plug two switches together and have a trunk form automatically. Modern networks have moved past this convenience because the security risk far outweighs the saved typing.

Why DTP is a serious security risk: An attacker can plug a rogue switch (or a Linux machine running the Yersinia attack tool) into an access port that still has DTP enabled. The rogue announces 'I am desirable' and the legitimate switch, still in 'dynamic auto' or 'dynamic desirable' mode by default, happily forms a trunk. The rogue now receives tagged frames for every VLAN on the network — the attacker is inside every broadcast domain simultaneously. This is called a switch spoofing attack.

How to disable DTP: switchport nonegotiate on an interface silences DTP frames entirely. You must issue this command on any interface where you care about security — which is every interface. On access ports, switchport mode access also effectively disables DTP negotiation as an attack vector (the port cannot be negotiated into trunk mode), but nonegotiate is the explicit belt-and-suspenders command.

DTP is not LACP: DTP negotiates trunking; LACP (and PAgP) negotiates EtherChannel. They are completely separate protocols running on separate ports for separate purposes. Do not confuse them on the exam.

hack.memory: DTP = Dangerous Trunking Protocol. Always disable it with switchport nonegotiate. The attack is called 'switch spoofing' — a rogue device announces desirable and pops into every VLAN. DTP is Cisco-only, sends every 30 seconds, and is ON by default on most access ports. If you forget nothing else: nonegotiate every port facing users.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: set SwA Gi0/1 to default (dynamic auto or desirable, depending on IOS). Connect a second switch with 'switchport mode trunk' — watch the trunk form automatically without you configuring SwA. Now on SwA type 'switchport nonegotiate' — the trunk drops because SwA no longer plays DTP. Add 'switchport mode trunk' and the static trunk comes back.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam will ask 'what command disables DTP on an interface?' (nonegotiate) and 'what attack does DTP enable?' (switch spoofing / VLAN hopping). Memorize both.

2.2.a.info:
DTP has four modes, and the exam absolutely expects you to know them cold, including the full negotiation matrix.

The four modes:

  • dynamic desirable — the port actively sends DTP frames trying to form a trunk. It initiates negotiation. Default on older Catalyst models (e.g., 3550).
  • dynamic auto — the port listens for DTP frames but does not initiate. It only forms a trunk if the other side is desirable or trunk. Default on newer Catalyst models (e.g., 2960, 3560).
  • trunk — the port is forced into trunk mode but still sends DTP frames. This is what switchport mode trunk alone gives you — secure against becoming an access port, but still advertising via DTP.
  • access — the port is forced into access mode and does not initiate DTP, but if it receives DTP frames from a desirable neighbor it can still be spoofed. Combine with nonegotiate for safety.

Separately, nonegotiate is a toggle that turns off DTP frame transmission on a port — it is orthogonal to the four modes above. switchport nonegotiate only works if the port is already in mode trunk or mode access (not dynamic).

The DTP negotiation matrix — memorize this:

  • desirable + desirable → trunk
  • desirable + auto → trunk
  • desirable + trunk → trunk
  • auto + auto → access (classic exam trap — no one initiates)
  • auto + trunk → trunk
  • trunk + trunk → trunk
  • access + anything → access (forced access always wins on its side)
  • nonegotiate + nonegotiate → whatever each side is forced to (static)

Easy rule: auto waits; it needs someone to ask. If both sides are auto, no one asks, and no trunk forms. Everything else involving desirable or trunk forms a trunk.

left.label: Initiates DTP
items: trunk — forced but still talks DTP

right.label: Waits / Silent
items: dynamic auto — waits to be asked

hack.memory: auto + auto = ACCESS. This is the most-tested DTP scenario on the planet. Both sides are wallflowers — neither asks, neither dances. Everything else involving 'desirable' or 'trunk' on at least one side = trunk forms. Mnemonic: 'Desirable dances with anyone (except access). Auto only dances if asked.'
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: build a single link between SwA and SwB. Cycle through mode combinations: both 'dynamic auto' (confirm with 'show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport' that Operational Mode = static access on both). Change SwA to 'dynamic desirable' — trunk forms instantly. Change SwB to 'access' — trunk drops, SwB forces access. Build a truth-table flashcard from this one lab.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17 walks through the full matrix with live labs. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. The exam will hand you two sides' configurations and ask 'will a trunk form?' Know the matrix cold — it is nearly free exam points if memorized.

2.2.a.info:
The auto + auto = access mode scenario is the single most common DTP exam trap and a frequent real-world outage cause.

What happens: Both switch ports are in dynamic auto (default on most modern Catalyst models). Each side is willing to form a trunk if asked, but neither side initiates the request. The link quietly stays in access mode in the default VLAN (VLAN 1). No trunk forms. No error. No warning. Traffic for VLAN 10 and VLAN 20 that was supposed to traverse this link is silently dropped or mixed into VLAN 1.

Why this matters on the exam: The classic CCNA scenario is 'SwA and SwB are connected. Both ports are in dynamic auto mode. VLAN 10 is configured on both. Why can PCs in VLAN 10 not ping each other across switches?' The answer is no trunk formed because both sides were auto. Fix: change at least one side to dynamic desirable or trunk.

Why this happens in real networks: A new switch is installed with default config. An admin connects it to an existing switch and assumes DTP will 'just work' because trunks have formed in the past. But the newer Cisco IOS defaults changed from 'dynamic desirable' (3550) to 'dynamic auto' (2960+) — now both sides default to auto and no trunk forms. The fix is a single command, but finding the bug requires knowing the default changed.

Diagnosis commands: show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport will show Administrative Mode: dynamic auto and Operational Mode: static access. The mismatch between admin (dynamic auto) and operational (static access) is the smoking gun. show interfaces trunk will return 'no trunk interfaces' — the port is not trunking.

Fix: switchport mode trunk on at least one side forces the trunk. Best practice: set both sides to switchport mode trunk + switchport nonegotiate and be done with DTP entirely.

hack.memory: Auto + Auto = ACCESS. Burn it into memory. Two wallflowers at a dance = no one dances. If the exam scenario says 'both ports are default / dynamic auto' and asks why VLAN 10 does not work across switches, the answer is 'no trunk formed.' Fix by making at least one side desirable or forced trunk.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: default both SwA Gi0/1 and SwB Gi0/1 (leave them at factory defaults — dynamic auto on 2960s). Create VLAN 10 on both, put PC1 on SwA VLAN 10 and PC2 on SwB VLAN 10. Ping — fails. Run 'show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport' and identify the Operational Mode = static access. Add 'switchport mode trunk' on SwA alone. Ping works now — trunk formed. This single lab cements the rule for life.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17 demonstrates this exact scenario. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Favorite exam trick: a topology diagram where both sides are labeled 'default config' or 'dynamic auto' and asks why inter-switch VLAN traffic fails. Answer: auto+auto = access mode, no trunk.

2.2.a.info:
show interfaces trunk is the single most important verification command for trunk operation. It is the first command you run whenever trunking is broken or you need to confirm your configuration took effect. Knowing how to read its output fluently is exam-critical.

The four-column output:

  • Port — the interface name (Gi0/1, Fa0/24, etc.).
  • Mode — administrative mode: on (forced trunk), desirable, auto, off, etc.
  • Encapsulation — 802.1q (always on modern switches), n-802.1q (negotiated), or isl (legacy).
  • Status — trunking means the port is actually operating as a trunk. Other values include not-trunking and other.
  • Native vlan — the VLAN whose traffic is sent untagged (default 1).

The three 'VLANs' sections below the header:

  • 'Vlans allowed on trunk' — the configured allowed list (what you typed with switchport trunk allowed vlan). Default: 1-4094.
  • 'Vlans allowed and active in management domain' — intersection of the allowed list AND the VLANs that actually exist in the VLAN database. If VLAN 20 is allowed but not created, it appears in the first list but not here.
  • 'Vlans in spanning tree forwarding state and not pruned' — the subset actually forwarding traffic right now (STP not blocking, VTP pruning not active).

Why show vlan brief does not help here: show vlan brief lists only access ports assigned to each VLAN. Trunk ports never appear in this output — a trunk carries multiple VLANs and cannot be listed under any single one. Students who run show vlan brief and do not see their trunk port often think the trunk is broken when it is working fine. Always use show interfaces trunk for trunk verification.

Related commands: show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport shows the same information plus Administrative vs Operational mode (useful for DTP troubleshooting). show dtp interface shows raw DTP state. show interfaces status shows a port summary with 'trunk' under the VLAN column for trunk ports.

children.name: Header: Port / Mode / Encap / Status / Native
Vlans allowed AND active (exist in VLAN DB)
Vlans in STP forwarding (actually passing traffic)

hack.memory: Trunks NEVER appear in 'show vlan brief' — they appear in 'show interfaces trunk'. The four key columns: Mode, Encap, Status, Native VLAN. The three VLAN list refinements: Allowed (configured) → Allowed & Active (exist) → STP Forwarding (actually passing). Each one is a tighter subset of the one above.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: build a trunk between SwA and SwB carrying VLANs 10, 20, 30. Create only VLAN 10 on SwB (skip 20 and 30 intentionally). Run 'show interfaces trunk' on SwB: 'Vlans allowed' shows 10,20,30 but 'Vlans allowed and active' shows only 10. Now create VLAN 20 on SwB — it appears in the active list. Shut VLAN 30 on SwA — it disappears from the forwarding list on SwB. Walk through each subset relationship with a live trunk.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam simlets show 'show interfaces trunk' output and ask 'which VLANs are actually forwarding on this trunk?' — that is the third subset (STP forwarding and not pruned). Practice reading real output until you can identify each subset instantly.

2.2.b.info:
802.1Q, commonly pronounced 'dot-one-Q' and often written dot1q in Cisco configs, is the IEEE open standard for VLAN tagging on Ethernet trunks. It is the only VLAN encapsulation method that matters on modern networks — every Cisco switch shipped in the last 15+ years uses 802.1Q exclusively, and every multi-vendor network depends on the standard.

What 802.1Q actually does: It defines a 4-byte tag inserted into the Ethernet frame header. The tag identifies which VLAN the frame belongs to, plus optional priority bits for QoS. Receiving switches read the tag, place the frame in the correct VLAN, and strip the tag when forwarding out an access port. The original frame payload is untouched — only the header changes.

802.1Q is an internal tagging method: the tag is inserted into the existing frame, between the Source MAC field and the EtherType field. This is a key architectural point. Ethernet frames on a trunk are still valid Ethernet frames — just with an extra 4 bytes in a specific location. Devices that understand 802.1Q know to look for the TPID 0x8100 and parse the tag; devices that do not may simply drop the frame as oversized.

802.1Q vs ISL (history lesson, not exam material): Cisco's earlier proprietary encapsulation was ISL (Inter-Switch Link), which wrapped the entire original frame in a new 26-byte ISL header plus a 4-byte ISL trailer. ISL was external encapsulation — the original frame was hidden inside a bigger envelope. 802.1Q won because it was an open standard with less overhead (4 bytes vs 30). ISL is obsolete and is not tested on the current CCNA 200-301 exam.

802.1Q in Cisco commands: On modern switches (2960, 9200) you never specify encapsulation — it is 802.1Q by default. On older multi-encap switches (3560), you type switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q before switchport mode trunk. On router subinterfaces for ROAS, the command is encapsulation dot1q 10 under interface Gi0/0.10 — telling the subinterface to tag/untag with VLAN 10.

hack.memory: 802.1Q = 'the VLAN sticker standard.' IEEE, open, 4 bytes, inserted between Source MAC and EtherType. Only encap that matters on modern exam. ISL = dead Cisco-only predecessor — know the name, forget the details. The 'dot1q' keyword shows up in switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q and encapsulation dot1q on ROAS subinterfaces.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: configure a router subinterface for ROAS — 'interface Gi0/0.10', 'encapsulation dot1q 10', 'ip address 10.0.10.1 255.255.255.0'. Repeat for VLAN 20. This is where 'dot1q' appears as a literal keyword. Then on a 3560 switch type 'switchport trunk encapsulation ?' — observe dot1q and isl as options. On a 2960, that same command is rejected because the switch only supports dot1q.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17 (trunks) and Day 18 (inter-VLAN routing / ROAS). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. The exam may ask 'which is an IEEE standard trunking encapsulation?' = 802.1Q. 'Which is Cisco-proprietary and obsolete?' = ISL. Quick recall, easy points.

2.2.b.info:
The 802.1Q tag is exactly 4 bytes (32 bits) inserted into the Ethernet frame between the Source MAC address and the EtherType/Length field. Nothing is added at the end of the frame — only in that specific middle position. The FCS (Frame Check Sequence) at the end is recalculated over the new frame to account for the added bytes.

The four fields inside the 4-byte tag — memorize the order and bit widths:

  • TPID (Tag Protocol Identifier) — 2 bytes / 16 bits. Fixed value 0x8100. This is what tells a receiving device 'this is a tagged frame.' When a switch sees 0x8100 where the EtherType field would normally be, it knows to parse the next 2 bytes as the tag control info.
  • PCP (Priority Code Point) — 3 bits. Values 0-7 used for QoS / Class of Service. Higher = higher priority. Voice traffic typically uses 5; network control uses 6-7.
  • DEI (Drop Eligible Indicator) — 1 bit. Signals that this frame can be dropped first under congestion (formerly called CFI — Canonical Format Indicator).
  • VID (VLAN Identifier) — 12 bits. The VLAN number 0-4095 (usable 1-4094). This is the most important field.

Together: 2B TPID + 2B (3+1+12 bits = 16 bits) of tag control information = 4 bytes total.

Where the tag goes in the frame — EXACT layout:

[Dest MAC 6B][Src MAC 6B][TPID 2B = 0x8100][PCP 3b][DEI 1b][VID 12b][EtherType 2B][Payload 46-1500B][FCS 4B]

Common exam misconception: Students sometimes think the tag is appended at the end of the frame. It is not — it is inserted in the middle, before the EtherType field. The 4 extra bytes push the EtherType, Payload, and FCS each 4 bytes further down the wire.

Why 4 bytes matter: The 4 bytes change the maximum frame size, require recalculation of the FCS, and must be handled by hardware that understands 802.1Q. Older or simpler hardware may drop the frame as malformed or oversized — which is why access ports strip the tag before sending to end hosts.

hack.memory: 4 bytes. Between Source MAC and EtherType. Breakdown = 16+3+1+12 bits. TPID is always 0x8100 — burn that constant in. Mnemonic for field order: 'TPD-V' = TPID, Priority (PCP), DEI, VID. 'T-P-D-V' sounds like 'TV' with extra letters.
hack.practice: Draw the frame format by hand every single day for a week: Dest MAC (6) → Src MAC (6) → TPID (2, 0x8100) → PCP (3 bits) → DEI (1 bit) → VID (12 bits) → EtherType (2) → Payload → FCS (4). Label bytes vs bits carefully. Quiz yourself: 'where does the tag go?' (between Src MAC and EtherType). 'How big?' (4 bytes). 'TPID value?' (0x8100).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8 — has a great byte-by-byte diagram. Exam tests exact bit widths and field order. Know 4 bytes = 2 TPID + 16 bits of control (3 PCP + 1 DEI + 12 VID). Also know the TPID constant 0x8100. These are pure memorization questions.

2.2.b.info:
The VLAN ID (VID) field inside the 802.1Q tag is 12 bits wide. That gives 2^12 = 4096 possible values (0 through 4095). Of those, two are reserved by the standard: 0 and 4095. The usable range is therefore 1 through 4094 — giving exactly 4094 usable VLANs.

Reserved values:

  • VLAN 0 — reserved. Indicates 'priority tag only' — a frame that carries 802.1Q's PCP field for QoS purposes but does not belong to any specific VLAN. Rarely used in modern networks.
  • VLAN 4095 — reserved. No defined use; blocked by the standard.

Cisco-specific ranges within 1-4094:

  • VLAN 1 — default VLAN. All access ports are in VLAN 1 by default. Cannot be deleted. Best practice: move production traffic off VLAN 1 and use it only as an unused default.
  • VLANs 2-1001 — Normal range. Fully supported by all Cisco switches and stored in the VLAN.DAT file on flash.
  • VLANs 1002-1005 — Reserved for legacy protocols (Token Ring, FDDI). Cannot be deleted or repurposed. Exam likes this detail.
  • VLANs 1006-4094 — Extended range. Supported on most modern switches but with caveats around VTP — extended-range VLANs cannot be managed by legacy VTP v1/v2 server mode. VTP v3 supports the extended range fully.

Why this matters on the exam: Expect a question like 'how many VLANs does 802.1Q support?' — the answer is 4094 (not 4096). Another common angle: 'what is the range of the normal VLAN range on Cisco switches?' — 1 to 1005, with 1002-1005 being reserved Token Ring/FDDI. And: 'what is the extended VLAN range?' — 1006-4094.

Practical note: Most production networks use far fewer than 4094 VLANs — typically a few dozen on a large enterprise campus. The 4094 limit is rarely a practical constraint but it is a favorite exam fact.

hack.memory: 12 bits = 4096 theoretical, minus 2 reserved (0 and 4095) = 4094 usable. Normal range = 2-1001. Reserved TR/FDDI = 1002-1005 (cannot delete). Extended = 1006-4094 (VTP v3 only in server mode). Exam answer: 4094, not 4096, not 4095.
hack.practice: On a Packet Tracer switch, try 'vlan 0' — rejected. 'vlan 4095' — rejected. 'vlan 1002' — already exists as fddi-default. 'vlan 2000' — works (extended range, but only in VTP transparent or v3 mode). Build a flashcard: VID field = 12 bits, usable = 1-4094, count = 4094, normal = 2-1001, extended = 1006-4094.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 16 (VLANs intro) and Day 17 (trunks). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. 'How many VLANs can 802.1Q support?' = 4094. Pure trivia, free points. Memorize the exact number.

2.2.b.info:
The 4-byte 802.1Q tag pushes the maximum Ethernet frame size from the standard 1518 bytes to 1522 bytes when tagged. This is important: a 1522-byte tagged frame is NOT a giant frame, jumbo frame, baby giant, or malformed frame — it is a perfectly valid 802.1Q-tagged frame and every 802.1Q-capable device handles it correctly.

The math in detail:

  • Standard Ethernet frame (no tag): 6B Dest MAC + 6B Src MAC + 2B EtherType + 46-1500B Payload + 4B FCS = up to 1518 bytes
  • Tagged Ethernet frame: 6B Dest + 6B Src + 4B 802.1Q tag + 2B EtherType + 46-1500B Payload + 4B FCS = up to 1522 bytes
  • The preamble (7B) and SFD (1B) are not counted in the Ethernet frame size by convention — they are part of the PHY layer.

Terminology exam students get wrong:

  • Baby giant = a frame slightly larger than 1518 bytes but not a true jumbo. 1522-byte tagged frames used to be called baby giants on older hardware that did not natively support 802.1Q.
  • Giant / Jumbo frame = typically 9000+ bytes. Used to reduce overhead on high-throughput links (storage, backup). Requires mtu 9000 configuration and must be supported end to end.
  • Runt = a frame smaller than the 64-byte minimum. Usually indicates a collision or bad hardware.

Why this matters in practice: In the early days of 802.1Q, some switches and NICs did not understand the larger tagged frames and would flag them as 'giants' or oversized errors. Modern hardware handles 1522-byte frames natively without any MTU adjustment. You may still see show interfaces counters labeled 'Giants' — on modern switches those count frames larger than the configured MTU (default 1500 for L3, 1518 for L2 non-tagged, 1522 for trunk ports).

Exam angle: 'What is the maximum frame size on an 802.1Q trunk?' = 1522 bytes. 'What happens to the frame size when an 802.1Q tag is added?' = increases by 4 bytes. 'Is a 1522-byte tagged frame a jumbo frame?' = NO, it is a standard tagged frame.

hack.memory: 1518 + 4 = 1522. Standard + tag = tagged max. NOT a jumbo (jumbo is 9000+). NOT an error. Just 4 extra bytes. Memorize: 1522 bytes is the magic trunk number. Baby-giant = old hardware term for 1519-1522 tagged frames.
hack.practice: Recite daily for a week: 1518 untagged, 1522 tagged, difference = 4 bytes (the 802.1Q tag). On a lab switch run 'show interfaces Gi0/1 | include MTU' — MTU is usually 1500 (payload), not the full frame. Check 'show interfaces counters errors' for 'giants' — on a trunk port, 1522-byte frames do not count as giants even though they exceed 1518.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam frequently asks 'max frame on a trunk?' = 1522. Also may ask 'what is the extra bytes added by 802.1Q?' = 4. Rote memorization, guaranteed points.

2.2.b.info:
PCP (Priority Code Point), also called CoS (Class of Service), is a 3-bit field inside the 802.1Q tag that carries Layer 2 QoS priority information. Values range from 0 (lowest priority) to 7 (highest). Eight total priority levels.

PCP vs DSCP — different layers: PCP is Layer 2 QoS — it only has meaning inside a tagged Ethernet frame on a trunk. DSCP (Differentiated Services Code Point) is Layer 3 QoS — it lives in the IP header's ToS byte and survives as the packet crosses routers (which strip and rewrite L2 headers). On the CCNA, PCP/CoS is the L2 number, DSCP is the L3 number. They can be mapped to each other but they are different fields in different headers.

Standard PCP value assignments (recommended by IEEE/Cisco):

  • 0 — Best Effort (default for normal user traffic)
  • 1 — Background (scavenger, bulk data)
  • 2 — Excellent Effort
  • 3 — Critical Applications
  • 4 — Video (streaming, conferencing)
  • 5 — Voice (VoIP, under 10ms latency)
  • 6 — Internetwork Control (routing updates)
  • 7 — Network Control (STP, LACP)

You do not need to memorize all eight, but 5 = voice is the most commonly tested because VoIP QoS is a real-world use case for CoS.

Where PCP matters in practice: When a trunk port is oversubscribed or congested, the switch can use the PCP value to decide which frames to transmit first and which to drop. Voice frames (PCP 5) get priority; best-effort data (PCP 0) can be queued or dropped. This requires QoS to be actively configured on the switch — PCP alone in the frame header does not make QoS happen; the switch must be told to honor it.

Untagged frames and PCP: An untagged frame has no PCP because there is no 802.1Q tag. On a trunk, native VLAN frames are untagged by default and therefore have no PCP. This is one reason voice VLANs are traditionally separate from the data/native VLAN — voice traffic always needs a tag so its PCP=5 is preserved.

hack.memory: PCP = 3 bits = 8 levels (0-7). Also called CoS (Class of Service) at Layer 2. PCP 5 = voice (memorize). PCP 0 = default/best-effort. Don't confuse with DSCP (Layer 3, 6 bits, lives in IP header). PCP is in the 802.1Q tag — no tag = no PCP = no L2 QoS.
hack.practice: On a Cisco switch with voice support: 'switchport voice vlan 20' on an access port. The attached IP phone tags voice frames with VLAN 20 + PCP=5 and sends data frames untagged in the native VLAN. Run 'show mls qos interface' or similar QoS verification command — observe how the switch trusts the CoS field from the phone. This shows PCP at work in the real world.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17 (covers PCP briefly) and Day 56 (QoS). Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 8 and 24. Exam may ask 'which field carries L2 QoS?' = PCP/CoS, 3 bits in the 802.1Q tag. Also 'which PCP value is used for voice?' = 5. Quick recall questions.

2.2.b.info:
ISL (Inter-Switch Link) was Cisco's proprietary VLAN trunking encapsulation, created before the IEEE 802.1Q standard existed. It is obsolete, no longer supported on modern Cisco hardware (2960, 9200, 9300), and not tested on the current CCNA 200-301 exam. You need to know only two things: the name, and that 802.1Q replaced it.

Historical context (skim, do not memorize): ISL was introduced in the mid-1990s on Catalyst 5000 and 6500 series switches. It worked by externally encapsulating the entire original Ethernet frame inside a new ISL header (26 bytes) and trailer (4-byte CRC), adding 30 bytes of total overhead. Contrast with 802.1Q's 4-byte internal tag — ISL was much heavier. ISL also did not support a native VLAN concept (every frame was encapsulated).

Why 802.1Q won: (1) IEEE open standard — works across all vendors, not just Cisco. (2) Much lower overhead (4 bytes vs 30). (3) Internal tagging is simpler and preserves the original frame structure. (4) Native VLAN support for backward compatibility. By the mid-2000s every new Cisco switch defaulted to 802.1Q, and by 2010 ISL was removed from all new platforms.

The one command you might see: On older multi-encap switches (Catalyst 3560, 3750, 4500, 6500), switchport trunk encapsulation ? would show both dot1q and isl as options. On modern switches the command is gone or only accepts dot1q. If you see switchport trunk encapsulation isl in a config, it is either very old or a lab exercise — not production.

Exam relevance: You may see one recall-level question: 'which is the Cisco-proprietary obsolete trunking encapsulation?' = ISL. Or 'which trunking encapsulation is an IEEE open standard?' = 802.1Q. That is the depth you need. Do not spend time memorizing ISL frame format.

hack.memory: ISL = Instantly Superseded Legacy. Cisco-only, external, 30 bytes overhead, dead. 802.1Q = IEEE, internal, 4 bytes, winner. Know the name, know it lost, move on.
hack.practice: Skip the lab — you cannot practice ISL on modern Packet Tracer reliably. Instead, memorize: ISL = Cisco + external + 30B + obsolete. 802.1Q = IEEE + internal + 4B + standard. One flashcard, done.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17 mentions ISL in passing. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8 has a historical note. CCNA 200-301 v1.1 does not test ISL configuration — only name recognition. Low-weight topic.

2.2.c.info:
The native VLAN is the one VLAN on an 802.1Q trunk whose frames are transmitted untagged. Every other VLAN's frames on the same trunk carry a 4-byte 802.1Q tag identifying their VLAN ID. Native VLAN frames skip the tag entirely and are placed in the native VLAN by receiving switches based on a simple rule: any untagged frame arriving on a trunk port belongs to the native VLAN.

The default native VLAN is VLAN 1 on all Cisco switches. This is a security concern — if both sides of every trunk default to VLAN 1 as native, any attacker who lands on VLAN 1 can potentially reach untagged traffic crossing every trunk. Best practice: change the native VLAN to an otherwise-unused VLAN (e.g., 999) on every trunk in production.

Why the native VLAN exists at all: Historical backward compatibility. In the early days of 802.1Q, some devices (old hubs, non-VLAN-aware switches, legacy endpoints) could not parse 802.1Q tags. They would receive tagged frames and drop them as malformed. The native VLAN concept allowed one specific VLAN's traffic to ride the trunk untagged so those legacy devices could still participate. This is rarely needed in modern networks, but the mechanism remains — and the security implications come with it.

Behavior rules:

  • Outbound: frames for the native VLAN leave the trunk untagged. Frames for any other allowed VLAN leave tagged.
  • Inbound: a frame arriving with a tag is placed in the VLAN indicated by the tag. A frame arriving without a tag is placed in the native VLAN — regardless of what the sender intended.

Verification: show interfaces trunk shows the Native VLAN column for every trunk. Each trunk port lists its configured native VLAN in the header table.

nodes: Untagged frame arrives → placed in native VLAN

hack.memory: Native VLAN = the ONE no-sticker VLAN per trunk. Default VLAN 1. Untagged frames on a trunk always land in the native VLAN. Best practice: change away from VLAN 1, use an unused VLAN like 999, and match it on both ends of every trunk. The native VLAN is the one exception to 'everything on a trunk is tagged.'
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: configure a trunk between SwA and SwB. Run 'show interfaces trunk' — Native vlan = 1. Change SwA to native VLAN 99 with 'switchport trunk native vlan 99' — verify with 'show interfaces trunk' again. Leave SwB at 1 (creating a mismatch — covered in next micro). Then correct the mismatch by setting SwB to native VLAN 99 as well. This establishes the 'native VLAN must match on both ends' intuition.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 8-9. Exam will ask 'what is the default native VLAN?' (1) and 'what happens to an untagged frame on a trunk?' (placed in native VLAN). Know these cold.

2.2.c.info:
Both ends of an 802.1Q trunk must agree on the same native VLAN. If they disagree, traffic leaks silently between two different VLANs in a way that creates connectivity bugs, security holes, and STP instability.

What happens on a mismatch — step by step:

  1. SwA is configured with native VLAN 1. SwB is configured with native VLAN 99.
  2. A host in VLAN 1 on SwA sends a broadcast. SwA places it on the trunk untagged (because VLAN 1 is native on SwA).
  3. The frame arrives at SwB with no tag.
  4. SwB applies its rule: 'untagged frame → native VLAN.' SwB places the frame in VLAN 99.
  5. The VLAN 1 broadcast has now leaked into VLAN 99. Any host in VLAN 99 on SwB receives it. Traffic has crossed broadcast domains.

Consequences:

  • Connectivity bugs: hosts appear to be able to talk to hosts in a different VLAN, or STP topology changes affect the 'wrong' VLAN on the far side.
  • Security: an attacker in VLAN 1 on SwA reaches VLAN 99 traffic on SwB without any routing or ACL evaluation.
  • STP: spanning tree runs per VLAN, and a native VLAN mismatch confuses STP's view of which link belongs to which VLAN — can trigger loops or block-state flaps.

How you find out: CDP actively detects this mismatch and logs %CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH on the console. STP may also log %SPANTREE-2-RECV_PVID_ERR. If you see either message, you have a native VLAN mismatch somewhere and must fix it.

Fix: Set both ends to the same native VLAN with switchport trunk native vlan 999. Confirm with show interfaces trunk on both switches — the Native vlan column must match.

hack.memory: Native VLAN MUST match on both ends. Mismatch = traffic leaks silently + CDP warning %CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH. The leak direction matters: SwA's native VLAN becomes SwB's native VLAN on arrival. Two different VLANs get bridged without routing. Fix: match the native VLAN on both sides.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: deliberately create a mismatch — SwA native=1, SwB native=99. Watch the console — CDP logs the warning within 60 seconds. Create a PC in VLAN 1 on SwA and a PC in VLAN 99 on SwB. Ping between them — they can actually communicate without any routing because the native VLAN mismatch bridged their broadcast domains. Scary in production, perfect for learning. Fix by matching native VLAN, and the unexpected connectivity disappears.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. This is a classic exam scenario — topology with different native VLANs and a trouble ticket. Recognize the CDP warning message and know the fix is to match native VLANs on both ends.

2.2.c.info:
switchport trunk native vlan [vlan-id] is the interface-level command that changes the native VLAN on a trunk port. Without this command, the native VLAN remains the default — VLAN 1. Best practice in production is to change it to an unused VLAN (commonly 999) on every trunk.

Syntax and example:

interface Gi0/1
 switchport mode trunk
 switchport nonegotiate
 switchport trunk native vlan 999
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30,999

Notice that the native VLAN is also included in the allowed VLAN list — if you omit it, the native VLAN's traffic is blocked from crossing the trunk. This is a common subtle mistake.

Three rules to follow for secure native VLAN configuration:

  1. Change the native VLAN away from VLAN 1. Anything unused — 999 is a common convention — is better than the default.
  2. Match the native VLAN on both ends. Both switches on either end of a trunk must be configured with the same native VLAN or you create a mismatch (see 2.2.c.2).
  3. Do not place any user hosts in the native VLAN. The native VLAN should have zero hosts. No access ports should assign hosts to VLAN 999 (or whatever you chose). This prevents the double-tagging attack (see 2.2.c.5) because no host can source traffic onto the native VLAN.

VLAN existence: The native VLAN must exist in the VLAN database on the switch. vlan 999 → name NATIVE-UNUSED before referencing it in switchport trunk native vlan 999. If the VLAN does not exist, the trunk still accepts the config but traffic behavior can be unpredictable.

Verification: show interfaces trunk displays the Native vlan column on every trunk. show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport shows 'Trunking Native Mode VLAN: 999' line for a single port.

hack.memory: The native VLAN config recipe: (1) Move away from VLAN 1. (2) Match both ends. (3) No hosts in it. (4) Include it in the allowed VLAN list or its traffic is blocked. Default command path: 'switchport trunk native vlan 999' under the trunk interface. Always verify with 'show interfaces trunk'.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: create VLAN 999 on both SwA and SwB with 'name NATIVE-UNUSED'. On each trunk port: 'switchport trunk native vlan 999'. Verify with 'show interfaces trunk' — Native vlan = 999 on both. Then omit 999 from 'switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20' — confirm that native-VLAN traffic still flows (IOS allows the native VLAN even if not in the allowed list on some platforms, but best practice is to include it explicitly). Build muscle memory for the full recipe.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam will test the command syntax and the best practice of using an unused VLAN. Also test the requirement to match on both ends — a common simlet scenario.

2.2.c.info:
When two directly connected Cisco switches have different native VLANs on the same trunk link, CDP detects it and logs a specific warning message: %CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH. This is the single most common troubleshooting clue that points directly at a native VLAN bug.

The exact log message format:

*Apr 16 14:22:15.231: %CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH: Native VLAN mismatch discovered on GigabitEthernet0/1 (1), with SwB GigabitEthernet0/1 (99).

Reading this: the local switch's Gi0/1 has native VLAN 1, and the neighbor (SwB) on its Gi0/1 has native VLAN 99. Both values are shown in parentheses. The severity is 4 (warning).

How CDP detects it: CDP advertisements (sent every 60 seconds by default) include a Native VLAN TLV among the many fields each switch announces to its neighbors. When SwA receives SwB's CDP frame, it compares SwB's advertised native VLAN against its own local native VLAN on the same port. If they differ, SwA logs the mismatch warning. Both switches log the warning independently from their own perspective.

Severity matters — this is a warning, not an error: CDP logs the mismatch but does not prevent the trunk from forming or block traffic. The trunk still works; frames still flow. But they flow between the wrong VLANs. This is why the warning is often ignored or missed — nothing appears broken until a user reports 'I can reach this server even though I'm supposedly not supposed to be able to.'

STP also detects mismatches: When Per-VLAN Spanning Tree (PVST+) is running and the native VLAN mismatches, STP may log %SPANTREE-2-RECV_PVID_ERR — a severity-2 error — because the PVID (VLAN ID) in the STP BPDU does not match the expected native VLAN. STP may also put the port into 'blocking' state for the inconsistent VLAN, partially protecting the network but also breaking traffic.

Troubleshooting workflow: See %CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH in syslog → run show interfaces trunk on both switches → compare Native vlan columns → fix the mismatched side with switchport trunk native vlan [id] → wait 60 seconds for CDP to re-validate → confirm warning stops.

nodes: CDP advert every 60s includes Native VLAN TLV

hack.memory: The magic warning string: %CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH. Severity 4 (warning). CDP tells you both the local and remote native VLAN values in parentheses. Trunk still works, but traffic leaks. Fix by matching. Also know the STP error: %SPANTREE-2-RECV_PVID_ERR (severity 2, more serious).
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: intentionally create a native VLAN mismatch. Wait up to 60 seconds and watch the console log the warning. Note: Packet Tracer formats the message slightly differently, but the key tag %CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH should appear. Fix by matching and confirm the warning stops on the next CDP cycle. Build the muscle of 'see warning → think native VLAN → check show interfaces trunk'.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17 and Day 34 (CDP). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam may present a syslog snippet with %CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH and ask 'what is the problem?' — answer: native VLAN mismatch on a trunk. Or 'which command shows the native VLAN?' = show interfaces trunk. Easy points if you recognize the log message.

2.2.c.info:
The double-tagging VLAN hopping attack is a classic Layer 2 attack that abuses the native VLAN's untagged nature to smuggle traffic from one VLAN into another — bypassing VLAN segmentation entirely. It is one of the most frequently tested security topics on the CCNA exam.

The attack, step by step:

  1. The attacker is in VLAN 1 (the default native VLAN — and the default for access ports).
  2. The attacker crafts an Ethernet frame with two 802.1Q tags stacked inside it: the outer tag is VLAN 1 (matching the native VLAN), and the inner tag is VLAN 10 (the target VLAN the attacker wants to reach).
  3. The frame enters the first switch's access port.
  4. The first switch sees the outer tag, but because VLAN 1 is the native VLAN on its trunk to the next switch, it strips the outer tag and sends the frame across the trunk untagged with respect to the native VLAN. But the inner tag (VLAN 10) is still inside the frame — it looks to the first switch like it is just the payload.
  5. The frame arrives at the second switch with one visible tag: VLAN 10.
  6. The second switch parses the VLAN 10 tag, assumes it is a legitimate tagged trunk frame, and delivers the frame into VLAN 10 on the far side.
  7. The attacker has successfully injected a frame into VLAN 10 from VLAN 1 — VLAN segmentation bypassed.

Critical limitation of the attack: it is one-way only. The attacker can send frames into the target VLAN but cannot receive responses because the return traffic cannot be double-tagged by the target host. This limits the attack to denial-of-service, packet injection (e.g., forged ARP), or exploiting services that respond in the target VLAN regardless. Despite the limitation, it is extremely dangerous.

Two mitigations — use both:

  1. Change the native VLAN to an unused VLAN (e.g., 999). Put zero hosts in it. If no attacker can be in the native VLAN, the outer-tag-matches-native trick fails at step 3 because the attacker cannot originate a frame whose outer tag matches the native VLAN.
  2. Enable vlan dot1q tag native globally. This tags even native VLAN traffic on trunks — eliminating the untagged pathway entirely. With this enabled, the first switch would see a double-tagged frame as anomalous (both tags present where it expects one) and drop it.

threats: Attacker in VLAN 1 builds frame: [outer VLAN 1][inner VLAN 10][payload]
defenses: Change native VLAN to unused 999 (no hosts)
No user ports in native VLAN

hack.memory: Double-tagging = two stickers on one frame. First switch peels off the outer sticker (because it matches the native VLAN and is expected to be stripped), exposes the inner sticker (target VLAN), and delivers to the wrong mailbox. Attack is ONE-WAY — attacker injects but cannot receive responses. Two fixes: (1) change native VLAN to unused, (2) 'vlan dot1q tag native' global.
hack.practice: Difficult to demo in Packet Tracer (requires Scapy or similar to craft double-tagged frames). Instead: draw the attack on paper — frame with two tags, switch strips the outer, inner tag is treated as legitimate. Confirm the mitigation logic: if the native VLAN is 999 and no attacker can be in 999, then the attack cannot start. If 'vlan dot1q tag native' is on, the first switch does not strip anything — the double-tag becomes visible and is dropped.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8-9. Favorite exam topic: 'how does an attacker hop VLANs?' (double-tagging). 'How do you mitigate it?' (change native VLAN + 'vlan dot1q tag native'). 'What is the limitation of the attack?' (one-way only). Memorize all three.

2.2.c.info:
vlan dot1q tag native is a global configuration command that tells the switch to tag even native VLAN traffic on trunks — eliminating the untagged pathway that double-tagging attacks exploit. After this command, every frame leaving a trunk port carries an 802.1Q tag, including frames in the native VLAN.

Why this mitigates double-tagging: The double-tag attack (covered in 2.2.c.5) depends on the first switch stripping the outer tag because it matches the native VLAN. With vlan dot1q tag native enabled, the switch no longer strips the outer tag — native VLAN traffic is tagged like every other VLAN. A double-tagged frame is treated as suspicious (both tags visible, neither implicitly 'the native') and is typically dropped by the hardware.

Command syntax and scope:

Switch(config)# vlan dot1q tag native

This is a global command, not an interface command. Once enabled, it affects every 802.1Q trunk on the device. There is no per-trunk version — it is all or nothing.

Caveats and compatibility:

  • Both ends should have it enabled. If only one side tags native traffic and the other expects it untagged, behavior varies — some platforms drop, some log a warning, some still accept. Configure it consistently on every switch in the L2 domain.
  • Old hardware may not support it. Legacy devices that cannot parse the tag on what they expect to be native/untagged traffic will drop frames. This is rarely an issue on modern networks.
  • Interaction with IP phones: Cisco IP phones using voice VLAN expect tagged voice frames and untagged data frames on the native VLAN. Enabling vlan dot1q tag native can break some phone configurations if the access port is trunking to the phone. Test before deploying in voice networks.

Verification: show vlan dot1q tag native or show running-config | include dot1q tag confirms whether the command is active. There is no per-frame way to verify native-VLAN tagging without a packet capture.

Defense-in-depth: vlan dot1q tag native is one of two recommended mitigations for VLAN hopping. The other is changing the native VLAN to an unused VLAN with no hosts. Use both for layered defense — changing the native VLAN alone is the easier and more common first step, but adding the tag-native command closes any residual gap.

threats: Double-tagging attack relies on native VLAN being untagged
defenses: All native traffic now carries 802.1Q tag
Attacker's outer tag no longer implicitly stripped

hack.memory: 'vlan dot1q tag native' = GLOBAL command. Tags even the native VLAN. Kills the double-tag attack by removing the untagged pathway. Best combined with changing native VLAN to an unused value (layered defense). Both sides must have it enabled — do NOT mix-and-match across a trunk.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: on both SwA and SwB in global config, type 'vlan dot1q tag native'. Verify with 'show running-config | include dot1q'. Traffic still flows on trunks — the command is invisible to normal operation. For the full security posture, pair it with 'switchport trunk native vlan 999' and create VLAN 999 as an unused container. Now the trunk has BOTH mitigations: unused native VLAN + native-VLAN tagging.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8-9. Exam asks 'which global command mitigates double-tagging by tagging native VLAN frames?' = vlan dot1q tag native. Also know it is global (not interface) scope. Medium-weight memorization topic.
